CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS _content_info (id TEXT PRIMARY KEY, "ready" BOOLEAN, "structureVersion" VARCHAR, "version" VARCHAR, "__hash__" TEXT UNIQUE); -- structure
INSERT INTO _content_info VALUES ('checksum_blog', false, 'HgiavfbRY-6HTrcyV6bFUbVpHh0Fu8-bBSQgJaL1Eok', 'v3.5.0--OtZY6lak3tcinV1-Ph_-UkIcOt2XKJnpvAW5deby6Qc', '4e-KKGZnpeNe-bWS1LWY7PYF4b7i6P31hXaH4polKFA'); -- meta
DROP TABLE IF EXISTS _content_blog; -- structure
CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS _content_blog (id TEXT PRIMARY KEY, "title" VARCHAR, "accentColor" VARCHAR NULL, "archivedAt" VARCHAR NULL, "author" VARCHAR NULL, "blocks" TEXT DEFAULT '[]', "body" TEXT, "canonical" VARCHAR NULL, "category" VARCHAR NULL, "coverAlt" VARCHAR NULL, "coverCaption" VARCHAR NULL, "coverImage" VARCHAR NULL, "coverPosition" VARCHAR NULL, "coverStyle" VARCHAR NULL, "date" VARCHAR NULL, "description" VARCHAR, "excerpt" VARCHAR NULL, "extension" VARCHAR, "lab" VARCHAR NULL, "mediaRefs" TEXT DEFAULT '[]', "meta" TEXT, "navigation" TEXT DEFAULT true, "noindex" BOOLEAN DEFAULT false, "ogDescription" VARCHAR NULL, "ogImage" VARCHAR NULL, "ogTitle" VARCHAR NULL, "path" VARCHAR, "readingTime" VARCHAR NULL, "seo" TEXT DEFAULT '{}', "seoDescription" VARCHAR NULL, "seoTitle" VARCHAR NULL, "slug" VARCHAR, "status" VARCHAR NULL, "stem" VARCHAR, "tags" TEXT DEFAULT '[]', "type" VARCHAR NULL, "updatedAt" VARCHAR NULL, "__hash__" TEXT UNIQUE); -- structure

Those enterprises that embed digital innovation into their strategic core are not just insulating themselves against the caprice of an ever-shifting marketplace; they are well-prepared to achieve significant growth and success."}},{"id":"block-1778524241523-5e1d42","type":"image","title":"img1","visible":true,"data":{"imageUrl":"/uploads/digicore1-60b82b69.webp","alt":"Digicore1 60b82b69","caption":"In a world where change is the only constant, organizations are rapidly pivoting from traditional business strategies to a “Digital Core First” approach. Here, business is not conducted; it is continuously reimagined and refined under the pervasive influence of digital integration."}},{"id":"block-1778532694971-b058dc","type":"heading","title":"","visible":true,"data":{"kicker":"","heading":"Data Lake vs. Real-Time Analytics","subheading":"","level":"h3"}},{"id":"block-1778532746127-c3f111","type":"text","title":"","visible":true,"data":{"heading":"","body":"we turn our gaze to two distinct protagonists, Organization A and Organization E, each charting their own course through the turbulent waters of digital integration. Here, through their journeys, we discern not just the mechanics of digital strategy but the artistry of its integration into the core business."}},{"id":"block-1778532784790-7ca557","type":"heading","title":"","visible":true,"data":{"kicker":"","heading":"Leveraging Real-Time Analytics in a Digital-Core Culture","subheading":"","level":"h4"}},{"id":"block-1778532795322-bad198","type":"text","title":"","visible":true,"data":{"heading":"","body":"Organization A, with a digital core, demonstrates an advanced application of technology in customer interaction management. The craft of conversation is not merely managed but transformed under the vigilant gaze of a text recognition algorithm, a brainchild of the company’s own product owner, who has woven sophistication into the fabric of a the chatbot. This algorithm, cradled within a cognitive service engine, listens and learns, processing the ebb and flow of dialogue with the finesse of a seasoned interlocutor."}},{"id":"block-1778534563800-e6d246","type":"image","title":"","visible":true,"data":{"imageUrl":"/uploads/digicore2-6e392119.webp","alt":"Digicore2 6e392119","caption":"The image depicts a Microsoft Azure-based data workflow. It starts with data ingestion via Event Hubs, storage in Azure Data Lake, processing with Azure Synapse Analytics, and visualization in Power BI. Azure Analysis Services models the data for analytics, while Azure Cosmos DB supports real-time applications, showcasing a full data lifecycle from collection to actionable insights.","layout":"contained"}},{"id":"block-1778535657778-da6035","type":"text","title":"","visible":true,"data":{"heading":"","body":"This real-time analysis system provides immediate feedback and assessment, generating valuable metrics that reflect shifts in customer satisfaction levels. By integrating this technology into their digital strategy, Organization A can promptly adjust and tailor their communication strategies, ensuring a more personalized and effective customer service approach. This real-time responsiveness is a clear advantage in maintaining high customer engagement and satisfaction."}},{"id":"block-1778535682524-fe8a9c","type":"heading","title":"","visible":true,"data":{"kicker":"","heading":"Navigating Data Lakes in a Traditional Core Setting","subheading":"","level":"h4"}},{"id":"block-1778535712168-a7519f","type":"text","title":"","visible":true,"data":{"heading":"","body":"Contrastingly, Organization E operates without a digital core, relying on traditional data management methods that lack real-time processing capabilities. This approach results in a significant delay between data collection and insight generation, which stifles the organization’s ability to respond promptly to market changes or customer needs. Over the years, they have amassed prodigious data lakes, reservoirs brimming with customer details and other nuggets of potential business wisdom, yet these remain largely untapped, obscured beneath the surface of their procedural lethargy."}},{"id":"block-1778535729697-f93bd8","type":"image","title":"","visible":true,"data":{"imageUrl":"/uploads/digicore3-25808430.webp","alt":"Digicore3 25808430","caption":"The concept of data mining is likened to delving into a mountain of information — sifting through vast seas of data and tables to unearth insights and forecasts. Without comprehensive information, the accuracy rates plummet.","layout":"contained"}},{"id":"block-1778535782498-478b8a","type":"text","title":"","visible":true,"data":{"heading":"","body":"However, without a modern analytical framework or tools, the value of this information often remains untapped. The company’s data analysts are likened to fortune-tellers or fishermen, who must navigate through these extensive data lakes in hopes of ‘catching’ relevant insights. This process is not only inefficient but also highly dependent on the skill and intuition of the analysts, leading to inconsistent results and potentially missed opportunities for leveraging data strategically."}},{"id":"block-1778535797449-dcac9f","type":"heading","title":"","visible":true,"data":{"kicker":"","heading":"To Wrap up..","subheading":"","level":"h3"}},{"id":"block-1778535808569-dde8d4","type":"text","title":"","visible":true,"data":{"heading":"","body":"The profound dichotomy between the lithe digital fluency of Organization A and the ponderous, archaic methodologies of Organization E underscores the quintessential strategic merit of real-time analytics. Where Organization A deftly navigates the shifting currents of customer demands, distilling satisfaction from the essence of immediacy, Organization E grapples with the sluggish shadows cast by its own analytical latency. This contrast not only illuminates the stark disparities but also paints a vivid tableau of the transformative potential residing within adept data maneuvering.\n\nThus, as businesses increasingly recognize the imperative of digital transformation, the choice between remaining with traditional data structures versus adopting innovative data analytics can be pivotal. The ability to instantly understand and react to customer data is no longer a luxury but a critical factor in a company’s operational success and competitive positioning in the digital age."}}]', '{"type":"minimark","value":[["h1",{"id":"untitled-blog-entry"},"Untitled Blog Entry"],["p",{},"A draft note for the Gribo archive. INSERT INTO _content_blog VALUES ('blog/blog/echoes-of-a-firestone-mapping-an-old-visitor.md', 'Echoes of a Firestone: Mapping an Old Visitor over Moray', 'coral', NULL, 'Gribo Digital', '[{"id":"block-1778551923519-e5e425","type":"text","title":"","visible":true,"data":{"heading":"","body":"Moray rises 3,500 m above sea level, some 50 km northwest of Cusco, where the high-Andean puna suddenly collapses into four concentric circular depressions; the largest is about 200 m in diameter and drops about 30 m deep. Each ring works like a climatic balcony: between rim and floor researchers have logged temperature gradients of 5–12 °C, creating up to twenty distinct “ecological floors” in just a few vertical metres."}},{"id":"block-1778551955234-fa6fcc","type":"image","title":"","visible":true,"data":{"imageUrl":"/uploads/mora1-28167c38.webp","alt":"Mora1 28167c38","caption":"Chroniclers after the conquest called Moray a “yachaywasi of the earth” — a living classroom where the Incas tested corn, quinoa, and chili peppers, adapting them to the empire’s varied climates.","layout":"contained"}},{"id":"block-1778551980971-d0023a","type":"text","title":"","visible":true,"data":{"heading":"","body":"Some local tales claim that Moray’s round cavities were not shaped only by Inca farm work or natural depressions, but are the scars of a meteor that, legend says, fell from the sky as a divine answer to Quechua prayers for fertile ground. Inside the ringed terraces we mapped six pits lined up from northeast to southwest; their spots (lat ≈ –13.32°, lon ≈ –72.19°) trace a path that, when projected into space, points to RA ≈ 195.4° and Dec ≈ –6.5°. That line — sharp as a kite string against the night — suggests an angled impact by a broken stone that tore through the air three to four thousand years ago.\n\nBeyond the usual tourist circuit exist three more craters on the same line. Visitors rarely see them; they sit off the marked paths and become clear only after long hikes across open fields or from the air. Their presence supports the idea of a small group of meteors whose many blows still shape the Cusco landscape."}},{"id":"block-1778552010045-a4deb1","type":"image","title":"","visible":true,"data":{"imageUrl":"/uploads/mora2-e99a75f4.webp","alt":"Mora2 E99a75f4","caption":"The intermediate dips show how the fragments gradually spread out.","layout":"contained"}},{"id":"block-1778552028658-9ba926","type":"heading","title":"","visible":true,"data":{"kicker":"","heading":"Reverse-orbit Rebuild","subheading":"","level":"h4"}},{"id":"block-1778552069270-541f23","type":"text","title":"","visible":true,"data":{"heading":"","body":"To rebuild the Sun-centered path of the rock that carved Moray’s dips, we used three plain starting points.\n\n1. Entry speed: about 20 km s⁻¹, the usual pace for falling meteors.\n2. Close pass by Earth: it brushed our planet’s path, so its nearest point to the Sun sits at 0.99 AU.\n3. Sky direction: the six impact spots line up with RA ≈ 195.4° and Dec ≈ –6.5°.\n\nAdding that entry speed to Earth’s own 29.78 km s⁻¹ gave the stone’s total speed around the Sun. A simple energy formula (the vis-viva rule) then set its main axis at about 1.8 AU, and Kepler’s third rule turned that into a round-trip time of roughly 2.4 years. All of this fits an Apollo-type path, one that cuts across Earth’s track again and again."}},{"id":"block-1778552137743-d78a5c","type":"image","title":"","visible":true,"data":{"imageUrl":"/uploads/mora3-ac3e45f7.webp","alt":"Mora3 Ac3e45f7","caption":"Top view of the solar system: the yellow line is the meteor’s long oval track, the dashed orange ring is Earth’s path, and the yellow cross marks the Sun. The orbit cuts Earth’s track at the point tied to Moray.","layout":"contained"}},{"id":"block-1778552153680-014c29","type":"text","title":"","visible":true,"data":{"heading":"","body":"The model shows a stretched oval path (eccentricity ≈ 0.45). The rock swings from almost Earth’s orbit at its closest point to the Sun (0.99 AU) out to 2.61 AU at its farthest. The orbit tilts about 25 ° above the ecliptic. It cuts through Earth’s plane near heliocentric longitude 195 °, and its perihelion points around 273 °, a layout that could bring it back near our planet again and again."}},{"id":"block-1778552159577-60e4b1","type":"image","title":"","visible":true,"data":{"imageUrl":"/uploads/mora4-a6befdf5.webp","alt":"Mora4 A6befdf5","caption":"","layout":"contained"}},{"id":"block-1778552219096-610898","type":"text","title":"","visible":true,"data":{"heading":"","body":"This oval path, about 1.8 AU around (roughly 1.62 billion km), cuts across Earth’s track every two years and just under five months. At its closest point to the Sun it almost brushes Earth’s orbit, while at its farthest it reaches the inner edge of the asteroid belt. These figures recall asteroid (12538) 1998 OH — an Apollo object with T ≈ 1.91 yr and i ≈ 24.5° — though our unknown visitor travels on a slightly wider, slower loop."}},{"id":"block-1778552224364-6dcb4c","type":"image","title":"","visible":true,"data":{"imageUrl":"/uploads/mora5-33e0c6f8.webp","alt":"Mora5 33e0c6f8","caption":"Asteroid 12538 (1998 OH) and Earth share the inner-solar-system view. The grey oval shows the asteroid’s path, cutting across Earth’s track near the Sun.","layout":"contained"}},{"id":"block-1778552259170-c53ea1","type":"heading","title":"","visible":true,"data":{"kicker":"","heading":"Timing a Return","subheading":"","level":"h4"}},{"id":"block-1778552273498-456458","type":"text","title":"","visible":true,"data":{"heading":"","body":"If the object had stayed in one piece, it would have crossed Earth’s path roughly 1,255 times since the impact date placed around 1000 BCE. Apollo-type orbits wear down over time: planetary pulls, gravity kicks, and heat cracks slowly disperse or redirect the body. So, while its early passes were likely regular, by now it may have fractured, slipped into a different shape, or left Earth’s neighborhood altogether.\n\n1. It may still exist as a single asteroid, visiting our skies every few cosmic years.\n2. It may have broken up into smaller meteor clusters, too faint for radar but still able to make some random fireballs.\n3. It may have vanished after many close calls, either burned away or pushed away of Earth’s track."}},{"id":"block-1778552300774-720598","type":"heading","title":"","visible":true,"data":{"kicker":"","heading":"Quick references","subheading":"","level":"h4"}},{"id":"block-1778552323803-d69ba1","type":"text","title":"","visible":true,"data":{"heading":"","body":"- Murray & Dermott, Solar System Dynamics, 1999.\n- NASA/JPL Small-Body Database, query 2025–05–03."}},{"id":"block-1778552333711-975f96","type":"quote","title":"","visible":true,"data":{"quote":"“The sky writes not with ink, but with light turned to stone; whoever reads those celestial runes hears the heartbeat of the Universe once more.”","attribution":"","variant":"editorial"}}]', '{"type":"minimark","value":[["h1",{"id":"untitled-blog-entry"},"Untitled Blog Entry"],["p",{},"A draft note for the Gribo archive. Shape the argument, keep the friction visible, and let the system explain what it is becoming."]],"toc":{"title":"","searchDepth":2,"depth":2,"links":[]}}', '', 'Draft', NULL, NULL, NULL, NULL, 'editorial-gradient', '2026-05-12', '“The sky writes not with ink, but with light turned to stone; whoever reads those celestial runes hears the heartbeat of the Universe once more.”', NULL, 'md', 'physics', '["/uploads/mora1-28167c38.webp","/uploads/mora2-e99a75f4.webp","/uploads/mora3-ac3e45f7.webp","/uploads/mora4-a6befdf5.webp","/uploads/mora5-33e0c6f8.webp"]', '{}', 'true', true, NULL, '/og/gribo-digital.png', NULL, '/blog/echoes-of-a-firestone-mapping-an-old-visitor', '3 min read', '{"title":"Echoes of a Firestone: Mapping an Old Visitor over Moray","description":"“The sky writes not with ink, but with light turned to stone; whoever reads those celestial runes hears the heartbeat of the Universe once more.”"}', 'Draft editorial note from the Gribo Digital archive.', 'Untitled Blog Entry | Gribo Digital', 'echoes-of-a-firestone-mapping-an-old-visitor', 'published', 'blog/echoes-of-a-firestone-mapping-an-old-visitor', '["echoes","firestone","mapping","visitor","over","moray"]', 'blog', '2026-05-12', '_WygiNnIjrWILABLtI3J7ud_nwwqILZSl1RynoL6nz8'); -- _WygiNnIjrWILABLtI3J7ud_nwwqILZSl1RynoL6nz8
INSERT INTO _content_blog VALUES ('blog/blog/openclaw-friction.md', 'When Agents Promise Autonomy but Deliver Friction', NULL, NULL, 'Gribo Digital', '[]', '{"type":"minimark","value":[["p",{},"The first promise of software agents is relief. The second, more difficult promise, is judgment."],["p",{},"When an interface claims autonomy but asks the user to supervise every fragile step, the result is not collaboration. It is a new kind of clerical labor wearing a futuristic label."],["p",{},"Friction is rarely just a bug. It is a design argument made visible: a trace of where the system still needs a human to carry its uncertainty."],["h2",{"id":"notes"},"Notes"],["ul",{},["li",{},"Autonomy should reduce cognitive switching."],["li",{},"Approval flows need clear stakes."],["li",{},"A good agent explains uncertainty without making the user manage its anxiety."]]],"toc":{"title":"","searchDepth":2,"depth":2,"links":[{"id":"notes","depth":2,"text":"Notes"}]}}', 'https://gribo.digital/blog/openclaw-friction', 'Interface Criticism', NULL, NULL, NULL, NULL, NULL, '2026-05-01', 'A short editorial note on agents, autonomy, product friction, and the hidden labor that appears when automation is not yet judgment.', NULL, 'md', 'ai', '[]', '{}', 'true', false, 'A note on the residue left when agents promise relief but make users manage the system''s uncertainty.', '/og/openclaw-friction.png', 'When Agents Promise Autonomy but Deliver Friction', '/blog/openclaw-friction', '4 min read', '{"title":"When Agents Promise Autonomy but Deliver Friction","description":"A short editorial note on agents, autonomy, product friction, and the hidden labor that appears when automation is not yet judgment."}', 'A Gribo Digital note on autonomous agents, product friction, and the hidden labor that appears when automation still needs supervision.', 'When Agents Promise Autonomy but Deliver Friction | Gribo Digital', 'openclaw-friction', 'draft', 'blog/openclaw-friction', '["agents","autonomy","friction"]', 'blog', '2026-05-08', 'QunD4TaMaWf0BWGZWVmT5xtG_UDf0RqMVHkFa_Qy_A4'); -- QunD4TaMaWf0BWGZWVmT5xtG_UDf0RqMVHkFa_Qy_A4

CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS _content_info (id TEXT PRIMARY KEY, "ready" BOOLEAN, "structureVersion" VARCHAR, "version" VARCHAR, "__hash__" TEXT UNIQUE); -- structure
INSERT INTO _content_info VALUES ('checksum_projects', false, 'Ya1fIzksAjPnUJ5lymykYpfUYupMis1Zksv70SXpYiU', 'v3.5.0--2cD7BU4_7cHap0C0I42idwlhRvrjaL3kjUsDFz04mlY', 'V8KZwTQYBM49idCdyxkGhgRpvAj1ghv8eaF-HeZq8tE'); -- meta
DROP TABLE IF EXISTS _content_projects; -- structure
CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS _content_projects (id TEXT PRIMARY KEY, "title" VARCHAR, "accentColor" VARCHAR NULL, "blocks" TEXT DEFAULT '[]', "body" TEXT, "canonical" VARCHAR NULL, "coverAlt" VARCHAR NULL, "coverCaption" VARCHAR NULL, "coverImage" VARCHAR NULL, "coverPosition" VARCHAR NULL, "coverStyle" VARCHAR NULL, "date" VARCHAR NULL, "description" VARCHAR, "docsFolder" VARCHAR NULL, "docsPath" VARCHAR NULL, "docsPaths" TEXT DEFAULT '[]', "extension" VARCHAR, "lab" VARCHAR NULL, "mediaRefs" TEXT DEFAULT '[]', "meta" TEXT, "navigation" TEXT DEFAULT true, "noindex" BOOLEAN DEFAULT false, "ogDescription" VARCHAR NULL, "ogImage" VARCHAR NULL, "ogTitle" VARCHAR NULL, "path" VARCHAR, "relatedArticles" TEXT DEFAULT '[]', "relatedBlogSlugs" TEXT DEFAULT '[]', "relatedDocs" TEXT DEFAULT '[]', "relatedPosts" TEXT DEFAULT '[]', "seo" TEXT DEFAULT '{}', "seoDescription" VARCHAR NULL, "seoTitle" VARCHAR NULL, "slug" VARCHAR, "stack" TEXT DEFAULT '[]', "status" VARCHAR NULL, "stem" VARCHAR, "summary" VARCHAR NULL, "tags" TEXT DEFAULT '[]', "type" VARCHAR NULL, "updatedAt" VARCHAR NULL, "year" INT NULL, "__hash__" TEXT UNIQUE); -- structure
INSERT INTO _content_projects VALUES ('projects/projects/astronomical-impact-trajectory-analysis.md', 'Astronomical Impact Trajectory Analysis', 'coral', '[]', '{"type":"minimark","value":[["h1",{"id":"astronomical-impact-trajectory-analysis"},"Astronomical Impact Trajectory Analysis"],["p",{},"Before this becomes a finished system, this dossier keeps track of what is being built, what is still uncertain, and which decisions leave a trace."]],"toc":{"title":"","searchDepth":2,"depth":2,"links":[]}}', '', '', '', '', 'center', 'editorial-gradient', '2026-05-12', 'A draft project dossier for an unfinished system.', 'astronomical-impact-trajectory-analysis', '/docs/Astronomical Impact Trajectory Analysis', '[]', 'md', 'physics', '[]', '{"projectIndex":"1. Project Overview\\n- What this project studies\\n- What this project does not claim\\n- Why Moray was selected as a case study\\n- Computational archaeology, orbital mechanics and speculative modeling\\n\\n2. Research Question\\n- Can a sequence of terrestrial points be treated as a possible impact trajectory?\\n- What can be inferred from geographic coordinates alone?\\n- What cannot be inferred without field measurements?\\n\\n3. Data Model\\n- Impact points\\n- Coordinate order\\n- Latitude and longitude\\n- Sample dataset\\n- Assumptions about point sequence\\n\\n4. Coordinate Systems\\n- Geographic coordinates\\n- ITRS: Earth-fixed reference frame\\n- ICRS: celestial reference frame\\n- Galactic coordinates\\n- Why astronomical time matters\\n\\n5. Trajectory Model\\n- Point sorting\\n- Cartesian conversion\\n- Direction vector\\n- Unit vector\\n- Vector averaging\\n- Projection distance\\n\\n6. Celestial Projection\\n- What the projection means\\n- What it does not mean\\n- RA and Dec\\n- Galactic longitude and latitude\\n- Plotly celestial map\\n\\n7. Orbital Elements\\n- Position vector\\n- Velocity vector\\n- Meteor velocity assumption\\n- Angular momentum\\n- Eccentricity vector\\n- Semimajor axis\\n- Inclination\\n- Ascending node\\n- Argument of periapsis\\n- True anomaly\\n- Mean anomaly\\n- Orbital type\\n\\n8. Application Architecture\\n- Streamlit interface\\n- Calculation engine\\n- Visualization engine\\n- Utility layer\\n- Export formats\\n\\n9. Code Walkthrough\\n- app.py\\n- trajectory_calculator.py\\n- visualization.py\\n- utils.py\\n\\n10. Moray Case Study\\n- Archaeological context\\n- Sacred landscape\\n- Agricultural engineering\\n- Circular depressions\\n- Why the site invites geometric interpretation\\n\\n11. Results and Interpretation\\n- Map output\\n- Celestial projection output\\n- Orbital elements output\\n- Why the results are exploratory\\n\\n12. Limitations\\n- Missing field data\\n- Missing crater geometry\\n- Missing geological evidence\\n- Simplified velocity model\\n- Atmospheric entry not modeled\\n- No N-body orbital reconstruction\\n\\n13. Future Work\\n- Drone photogrammetry\\n- DEM/topographic models\\n- Crater depth measurements\\n- Mineralogical testing\\n- Monte Carlo uncertainty simulation\\n- Comparison with known meteorite trajectories\\n- Integration with JPL Horizons or ephemeris data\\n\\n14. References\\n- Astropy\\n- NASA/JPL\\n- Kepler\\n- Newton\\n- Moray archaeological sources","projectIndexIntro":"## 1. Project Overview\n- What this project studies\n- What this project does not claim\n- Why Moray was selected as a case study\n- Computational archaeology, orbital mechanics and speculative modeling\n\n## 2. Research Question\n- Can a sequence of terrestrial points be treated as a possible impact trajectory?\n- What can be inferred from geographic coordinates alone?\n- What cannot be inferred without field measurements?\n\n## 3. Data Model\n- Impact points\n- Coordinate order\n- Latitude and longitude\n- Sample dataset\n- Assumptions about point sequence\n\n## 4. Coordinate Systems\n- Geographic coordinates\n- ITRS: Earth-fixed reference frame\n- ICRS: celestial reference frame\n- Galactic coordinates\n- Why astronomical time matters\n\n## 5. Trajectory Model\n- Point sorting\n- Cartesian conversion\n- Direction vector\n- Unit vector\n- Vector averaging\n- Projection distance\n\n## 6. Celestial Projection\n- What the projection means\n- What it does not mean\n- RA and Dec\n- Galactic longitude and latitude\n- Plotly celestial map\n\n## 7. Orbital Elements\n- Position vector\n- Velocity vector\n- Meteor velocity assumption\n- Angular momentum\n- Eccentricity vector\n- Semimajor axis\n- Inclination\n- Ascending node\n- Argument of periapsis\n- True anomaly\n- Mean anomaly\n- Orbital type\n\n## 8. Application Architecture\n- Streamlit interface\n- Calculation engine\n- Visualization engine\n- Utility layer\n- Export formats\n\n## 9. Code Walkthrough\n- app.py\n- trajectory_calculator.py\n- visualization.py\n- utils.py\n\n## 10. Moray Case Study\n- Archaeological context\n- Sacred landscape\n- Agricultural engineering\n- Circular depressions\n- Why the site invites geometric interpretation\n\n## 11. Results and Interpretation\n- Map output\n- Celestial projection output\n- Orbital elements output\n- Why the results are exploratory\n\n## 12. Limitations\n- Missing field data\n- Missing crater geometry\n- Missing geological evidence\n- Simplified velocity model\n- Atmospheric entry not modeled\n- No N-body orbital reconstruction\n\n## 13. Future Work\n- Drone photogrammetry\n- DEM/topographic models\n- Crater depth measurements\n- Mineralogical testing\n- Monte Carlo uncertainty simulation\n- Comparison with known meteorite trajectories\n- Integration with JPL Horizons or ephemeris data\n\n## 14. References\n- Astropy\n- NASA/JPL\n- Kepler\n- Newton\n- Moray archaeological sources","projectMemory":"This project studies the possibility of converting selected geographic points into a hypothetical impact trajectory and projecting that trajectory into celestial space.\\n\\nIt does not prove that Moray or any archaeological structure was created by a meteorite impact.\\n\\nMoray is selected because it is a geometrically, historically and symbolically rich Andean site whose circular terraces invite questions about land, sky, design and interpretation.\\n\\nThe project combines computational archaeology, orbital mechanics and speculative modeling to create a transparent research workflow. Its strongest contribution is not a final answer. Its strongest contribution is the method: a way to transform a fragile hypothesis into a visible, auditable and improvable computational process.","projectMemoryBody":"test","projectMemoryIntro":"test","projectMemoryTitle":"test","projectOverviewBody":"test","projectOverviewTitle":"test"}', 'true', true, NULL, '/og/gribo-digital.png', NULL, '/projects/astronomical-impact-trajectory-analysis', '[]', '[]', '[]', '[]', '{"title":"Astronomical Impact Trajectory Analysis","description":"A draft project dossier for an unfinished system."}', 'Draft editorial note from the Gribo Digital archive.', 'Astronomical Impact Trajectory Analysis | Gribo Digital', 'astronomical-impact-trajectory-analysis', '["Python"]', 'draft', 'projects/astronomical-impact-trajectory-analysis', 'A draft project dossier for an unfinished system.', '[]', 'project', '2026-05-12', 2026, 'NeR9hYqETR0UXQiE4etIuHYhjjY-EBqepcAhdf-IHOI');

UPDATE _content_info SET ready = true WHERE id = 'checksum_projects'; -- meta
CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS _content_info (id TEXT PRIMARY KEY, "ready" BOOLEAN, "structureVersion" VARCHAR, "version" VARCHAR, "__hash__" TEXT UNIQUE); -- structure
INSERT INTO _content_info VALUES ('checksum_docs', false, 'ikhwkEO43qpXZKvFHiSSKrkBOvOqnzybQBnJjInMINk', 'v3.5.0--o5DA0mIqL_TXxQYwX5LOoXnDuiEBxPh67_CLCVZrG8Y', 'wh8xtI2aGfhPLliTKNmKNvKR49h6nMgLpkkQwEgPKSc'); -- meta
DROP TABLE IF EXISTS _content_docs; -- structure
CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS _content_docs (id TEXT PRIMARY KEY, "title" VARCHAR, "accentColor" VARCHAR NULL, "blocks" TEXT DEFAULT '[]', "body" TEXT, "canonical" VARCHAR NULL, "coverAlt" VARCHAR NULL, "coverCaption" VARCHAR NULL, "coverImage" VARCHAR NULL, "coverPosition" VARCHAR NULL, "coverStyle" VARCHAR NULL, "date" VARCHAR NULL, "description" VARCHAR, "docsFolder" VARCHAR NULL, "extension" VARCHAR, "lab" VARCHAR NULL, "mediaRefs" TEXT DEFAULT '[]', "meta" TEXT, "navigation" TEXT DEFAULT true, "noindex" BOOLEAN DEFAULT false, "ogDescription" VARCHAR NULL, "ogImage" VARCHAR NULL, "ogTitle" VARCHAR NULL, "order" INT DEFAULT 0, "path" VARCHAR, "project" VARCHAR NULL, "projectSlug" VARCHAR NULL, "section" VARCHAR NULL, "seo" TEXT DEFAULT '{}', "seoDescription" VARCHAR NULL, "seoTitle" VARCHAR NULL, "slug" VARCHAR, "status" VARCHAR NULL, "stem" VARCHAR, "tags" TEXT DEFAULT '[]', "updatedAt" VARCHAR NULL, "__hash__" TEXT UNIQUE); -- structure
INSERT INTO _content_docs VALUES ('docs/docs/Astronomical Impact Trajectory Analysis/index.md', 'Project Overview', 'coral', '[{"id":"block-1778553486763-cdfbb6","type":"heading","title":"","visible":true,"data":{"kicker":"","heading":"What this project studies","subheading":"","level":"h3"}},{"id":"block-1778553496427-e368f6","type":"text","title":"","visible":true,"data":{"heading":"","body":"Astronomical Impact Trajectory Analysis is an exploratory computational project that studies how a sequence of geographic points on Earth can be transformed into a possible impact trajectory and projected toward a celestial reference frame.\n\nThe project begins with a speculative but structured question:\n\nIf a set of points on Earth were interpreted as possible traces of an ancient impact event, could we calculate a hypothetical direction of arrival and project that direction back into the sky?\n\nTo explore this question, the system takes latitude and longitude coordinates, converts them into spatial Earth-based coordinates, calculates a trajectory vector, and then projects that vector outward into celestial coordinate systems. From there, the model can estimate approximate orbital elements under simplified assumptions.\n\nThe project is not simply drawing a line over a map. It creates a computational chain that connects terrestrial geography, vector mathematics, astronomical coordinate systems and orbital mechanics."}}]', '{"type":"minimark","value":[["h1",{"id":"project-overview"},"Project Overview"],["p",{},"This draft page should explain the technical context first: setup, decisions, constraints, and the part of the system it helps remember."]],"toc":{"title":"","searchDepth":2,"depth":2,"links":[]}}', '', 'Mora2 E99a75f4', '', '/uploads/mora2-e99a75f4.webp', 'center', 'editorial-gradient', '2026-05-12', 'A computational research project that explores how selected geographic points on Earth can be transformed into a hypothetical impact trajectory, projected toward celestial coordinates, and interpreted through mathematics, physics, archaeology and speculative modeling.', 'astronomical-impact-trajectory-analysis', 'md', 'physics', '["/uploads/mora2-e99a75f4.webp"]', '{}', 'true', true, NULL, '/og/gribo-digital.png', NULL, 99, '/docs/astronomical-impact-trajectory-analysis', 'Astronomical Impact Trajectory Analysis', 'astronomical-impact-trajectory-analysis', 'Draft', '{"title":"Project Overview","description":"A computational research project that explores how selected geographic points on Earth can be transformed into a hypothetical impact trajectory, projected toward celestial coordinates, and interpreted through mathematics, physics, archaeology and speculative modeling."}', 'Draft editorial note from the Gribo Digital archive.', 'Project Overview | Gribo Digital', 'project-overview', 'draft', 'docs/Astronomical Impact Trajectory Analysis/index', '[]', '2026-05-12', '60LA5CE8I_sTkRw-2rLOjHSROzDzzkoes_UOBQNy-BA'); -- 60LA5CE8I_sTkRw-2rLOjHSROzDzzkoes_UOBQNy-BA

CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS _content_info (id TEXT PRIMARY KEY, "ready" BOOLEAN, "structureVersion" VARCHAR, "version" VARCHAR, "__hash__" TEXT UNIQUE); -- structure
INSERT INTO _content_info VALUES ('checksum_labs', false, 'IA7318SIMAnuJVIwwKXY6-D649IlKXot3TzcLsBVdR8', 'v3.5.0--VWvAkOp_4kqF5bcNfOE9H0Wka9wlKvxhLz4sORA_iyE', 'dbBXautSkVTj61kWV4sLC6xJvxaFHZzLkY2_W5uxTjY'); -- meta
DROP TABLE IF EXISTS _content_labs; -- structure
